learn.title: Hydrogen‑Based Green Steel
learn.intro: Decarbonising steel is crucial because the sector emits 7‑9% of global CO₂. Replacing coal with green hydrogen in blast furnaces can cut emissions dramatically, paving the way for a low‑carbon infrastructure and jobs while meeting growing demand for steel.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a blast furnace as a giant kitchen: instead of using charcoal (coal) to heat iron ore, green hydrogen acts like a clean gas stove, delivering 100 MJ of heat per kilogram without CO₂, similar to burning natural gas but with water vapor as the only by‑product.
learn.c2_text: In DRI, hydrogen reacts with iron ore to strip away oxygen, forming water. This reduces iron at about 800 °C, far lower than traditional coke‑based furnaces, saving energy and allowing easier integration with renewable electricity for hydrogen production.
learn.c3_text: India’s steel output reached 118 Mt in 2023. The government’s National Steel Mission targets 300 Mt by 2030, prompting pilots like JSW’s hydrogen‑based DRI plant in Karnataka, aiming to cut 2 Mt CO₂ annually.
learn.stat2: CO₂ emissions from steel: ~1.8 Gt CO₂/yr
learn.stat3: IEA: 30‑40% emissions reduction possible with hydrogen by 2050
learn.stat4: Green hydrogen cost target: $1.5‑$2 per kg by 2030

learn.title: Safer, Denser Solid‑State Batteries
learn.intro: Solid‑state batteries promise up to three times the energy density of lithium‑ion packs while eliminating flammable liquid electrolytes, a game‑changer for electric vehicles and grid storage in the clean‑energy transition.
learn.c1_text: Think of a solid‑state battery like a frozen lake versus a river. The solid electrolyte (the lake) doesn’t flow, preventing leaks and short‑circuits, allowing the use of lithium metal anodes that store up to 400 Wh/kg versus 250 Wh/kg in conventional cells.
learn.c2_text: By removing the separator and using a thin solid ceramic layer, cells can be packed tighter, delivering ~500 Wh per kilogram, enough for a 600‑km EV range on a single charge, while charging 30% faster than today’s packs.
learn.c3_text: India’s Department of Science & Technology funded a ₹1,200 crore solid‑state battery consortium in 2022, targeting pilot production by 2026 to support the nation’s goal of 30 million EVs by 2030.
learn.stat3: DOE: 5‑year target 500 Wh/kg for automotive batteries

learn.title: Small Wind Turbines for Homes
learn.intro: Distributed wind power lets households and villages generate clean electricity on‑site, reducing grid dependence and lowering bills, especially in remote or off‑grid areas where utility lines are costly.
learn.c1_text: Picture a garden fan turned upside down: the blades spin when wind passes, driving a generator. A 5 kW turbine can produce about 12,000 kWh per year with an average wind speed of 5 m/s—enough for a typical home’s electricity use.
learn.c2_text: The rotor connects to a gearbox (or direct‑drive) that speeds up rotation for the generator. Electricity then passes through a charge controller, inverter, and may be stored in batteries, making it usable for lights, appliances, or feeding back to the grid.
learn.c3_text: In Gujarat’s Kutch region, community‑owned 10 kW turbines supply 30% of village power, supported by the Ministry of New & Renewable Energy’s subsidy of up to 30% for turbines under 100 kW.
learn.stat3: DOE subsidy: up to $2,000 per kW installed

learn.title: Second‑Life EV Batteries for Grid Storage
learn.intro: After reaching ~80% capacity, EV batteries still hold substantial storage value. Repurposing them for grid applications extends their life, reduces waste, and provides cost‑effective energy storage for renewables.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a used smartphone battery still powering a flashlight. Similarly, a 60 kWh EV pack can be re‑configured into a stationary 40 kWh system, delivering 5‑10 years of grid‑scale service for peak shaving or renewable smoothing.
learn.c2_text: Modules are tested for remaining capacity, then re‑wired into larger arrays with battery management systems that balance charge, monitor temperature, and ensure safety—essential for utility‑scale reliability.
learn.c3_text: In 2023, Delhi’s Delhi Metro partnered with a local startup to install 2 MWh of second‑life batteries, cutting peak demand by 15% and earning incentives under India’s Energy Storage Mission.
learn.stat3: IRENA: 10 GW second‑life capacity possible by 2030
learn.stat4: India aims for 5 GW storage by 2030, 20% from repurposed EV packs

learn.title: Hydrogen Fuel Cells in Vehicles
learn.intro: Fuel‑cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) emit only water, offering long range and quick refueling. As hydrogen production turns greener, FCEVs can become a cornerstone of zero‑emission transport.
learn.c1_title: ⚡ How a Fuel Cell Generates Power
learn.c1_text: Think of a fuel cell as a reverse battery: hydrogen enters the anode, splits into protons and electrons. Electrons travel through an external circuit (producing electricity), while protons pass through a membrane to combine with oxygen at the cathode, forming water.
learn.c2_text: A typical 100 kW fuel‑cell stack can propel a midsize car for 500 km on 5 kg of hydrogen, with about 60% electrical efficiency—higher than internal combustion engines (≈25%).
learn.c3_text: India’s National Hydrogen Energy Mission supports 1,000 FCEV deployments by 2027, with stations in Delhi, Mumbai, and Bengaluru offering hydrogen at ₹150 per kg, aiming to cut transport emissions by 5 Mt CO₂ annually.
learn.stat3: DOE: 0.5 kg H₂ per 100 km for passenger cars
learn.stat4: India: 1,500 kg H₂ produced from renewables (2024)

learn.title: Microgrids for Village Resilience
learn.intro: Microgrids empower remote communities to generate, store, and manage their own electricity, enhancing energy independence and safeguarding power during natural disasters or grid failures.
learn.c1_text: Picture a neighborhood with its own mini‑grid: solar panels, wind turbines, and batteries are linked by a controller that can operate autonomously or sync with the main grid, delivering reliable power even when the larger network is down.
learn.c2_text: Advanced inverters balance supply and demand, while smart controllers prioritize critical loads (e.g., clinics) and dispatch stored energy. Grid‑forming inverters allow islanded operation without external frequency reference.
learn.c3_text: In Rajasthan’s Thar Desert, a 500 kW solar‑plus‑battery microgrid powers 1,200 homes, reducing diesel use by 1,800 MWh per year and supported by the Ministry of Power’s Integrated Power Development Scheme.
learn.stat3: Disaster‑resilient microgrids cut outage time by 80%
